teamObjective.explanation: My hand is weak (only non-trump 3-6 clubs) and an opponent is down to 2 cards, so the priority is to avoid wasting any useful card and let the trick pass as cheaply as possible.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 cards; I have no information about their strength, but with 3 cards they might be able to take control if I lead low.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents collectively hold 5 cards; player 1 has only 2 cards and may be close to finishing, but I cannot know whether they can beat a low club lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs expends the weakest card, keeps the slightly higher 4-6 clubs for later, and gives partner a chance to win the trick without committing any of my limited strength.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Still weak; playing the 4 first would leave the 3 as a stranded low card and waste a slightly better card.
The 5 is one of my better cards; leading it now gives up mid-strength without increasing the chance of winning the trick.
The 6 is my highest card; leading it would sacrifice my best remaining card when opponents likely have higher or trump cards to beat it.

risks: Opponent player 1 may be able to beat the 3 with a low trump or higher non-trump and continue toward finishing.
Partner may be unable to take the trick, leaving opponents in control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the absolute lowest card I preserve my small amount of card strength and avoid helping opponents win the current trick with unnecessarily high value.

notes: Opening lead with a weak, non-trump hand; no bombs or wildcards available. The safest play is to discard the lowest card.
